old_claim.note: Merchant Courier Networks provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Merchant Courts are a related commercial dispute-enforcement context for clothier/out-worker debts and market obligations, not a strict production method.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract highlights textile-related debt litigation in the Court of Common Pleas as evidence for the clothier and out-worker relationship; Britannica's law merchant entry supplies the broader commercial-dispute context.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The sources connect putting-out evidence to commercial debt litigation and merchant-law rules around contracts and buying/selling.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Commercial dispute enforcement is better supported than courier networks, but retained as a common dependency because the source is general legal context rather than a strict putting-out method.

ontology_before: Putting-Out Textile System depended on Merchant Courier Networks, implying a messaging/logistics mechanism that the source does not document.
ontology_after: Putting-Out Textile System depends on Merchant Courts as a contextual commercial dispute-enforcement layer for debts and obligations between clothiers and out-workers.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: putting_out_textile_system now depends on merchant_courts and no longer directly depends on merchant_courier_networks.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to courier/message logistics rather than clothier/out-worker production relationships.
A stronger source showed courier networks were the documented core mechanism behind late-medieval putting-out textile production.
The merchant_courts node were narrowed away from contracts, debts, buying, selling, or commercial dispute enforcement.
short_reason: Putting-out debt evidence fits commercial dispute enforcement better than courier networks.
